title: Arrays & Loops
description: Typed arrays are the backbone of interview problems — master declaring, accessing, and iterating them with proper TypeScript annotations to write confident, bug-free solutions.

sections.title: Typed arrays
sections.explanation: TypeScript offers two equivalent syntaxes for typed arrays: `number[]` and `Array<number>`. The shorthand `number[]` is more common in interviews. Always annotate array types — it catches wrong-element-type bugs at compile time rather than runtime.

exercise.prompt: Create a `number[]` called `scores` with values [85, 92, 78, 95]. Create a `string[]` called `words` with ["hello", "world"]. Print the length of each array, then print the first element of each.
exercise.starterCode:
const scores: number[] = [85, 92, 78, 95]
const words: string[] = 

console.log(scores.length)
console.log(words.length)
console.log(scores[0])
console.log(words[0])

validation.message: Print lengths (4, 2) then first elements (85, hello)

exercise.hint: Assign words = ["hello", "world"]. Array .length gives the count, [0] accesses the first element.

sections.title: Access, push, and destructure
sections.explanation: In interviews you constantly index into arrays, push new elements, and destructure results. TypeScript ensures you only push values matching the array's element type. Destructuring with type annotations extracts multiple values cleanly.

exercise.prompt: Create a `number[]` with [5, 10, 15]. Push 20 onto it. Destructure the first two elements into `a` and `b`. Print `a`, `b`, the array length, and the last element.
exercise.starterCode:
const nums: number[] = [5, 10, 15]
nums.push(___)

const [a, b] = nums
console.log(a)
console.log(b)
console.log(nums.length)
console.log(nums[nums.length - 1])

validation.message: Use push to add and destructuring to extract

exercise.hint: Push 20 onto the array. Destructuring [a, b] grabs the first two elements. nums.length - 1 indexes the last.

sections.title: for and for-of loops
sections.explanation: The classic `for` loop gives you index access, essential for two-pointer and sliding-window patterns. `for...of` iterates values directly and is cleaner when you don't need the index. TypeScript infers the loop variable type from the array.

exercise.prompt: Given `nums: number[] = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]`, use a classic for loop to print each element doubled. Then use a for-of loop to print each element as-is.

validation.message: First five lines are doubled values, next five are originals
Use a classic for loop and a for-of loop

exercise.hint: Multiply by 2 in the classic for loop. The for-of loop prints val directly.

sections.title: map, filter, reduce with types
sections.explanation: Functional array methods are concise and type-safe in TypeScript. `map` transforms each element, `filter` selects elements, and `reduce` accumulates a result. TypeScript infers callback parameter types from the array, but you can add annotations for clarity.

exercise.prompt: Given `nums: number[] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]`, use `map` to create `squares` (each number squared), `filter` to create `odds` (odd numbers only), and `reduce` to compute `sum`. Print each result.

validation.message: Use map, filter, and reduce methods

exercise.hint: Square with n * n, filter odd with n % 2 !== 0, reduce starting from 0.
